1: The divine command to love again a woman who is loved by another and is an adulteress -- 'just as YHWH loves the children of Israel' -- is the OT's most direct statement of the love that persists through unfaithfulness. In Christ this love becomes the explicit standard: 'God shows his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us' (Romans 5:8). The adulterous woman of Hosea 3 is the type of all who come to Christ having given their loyalty to other masters: 'You also have died to the law through the body of Christ, so that you may belong to another, to him who has been raised from the dead' (Romans 7:4). The love that Hosea is commanded to sustain is the love that Christ demonstrates on the cross.
2: Hosea's purchase of the unfaithful woman for fifteen pieces of silver and barley is a type of the redemption Christ accomplishes. The redemption price points to Christ's blood as the ransom: 'You were ransomed from the futile ways inherited from your forefathers, not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish or spot' (1 Peter 1:18-19). Hosea pays a price for Gomer in her degraded state; Christ pays the ultimate price for those degraded by sin. The arithmetic of redemption (fifteen pieces of silver, less than a slave's price) signals the humiliated condition of the one redeemed -- Christ dies for those who have forfeited every claim on value.
3: The terms Hosea sets -- stay with me, no prostitution, no other man, and even Hosea himself will wait -- describe a period of waiting and purification before full marital restoration. This is a shadow of the inter-advent period: Christ has redeemed his church (cross), claimed her, but the full consummation of the marriage awaits his return (Revelation 19:7-9: 'the marriage of the Lamb has come, and his Bride has made herself ready'). The church is betrothed but the wedding feast is future; the waiting period demands faithfulness (no prostitution to other lords) while the bridegroom is away (cf. Matthew 25:1-13, the virgins waiting for the bridegroom).
4: The many days without king, ruler, sacrifice, pillar, ephod, or teraphim describe the suspension of Israel's political and cultic institutions during exile. This suspension is a shadow of the new covenant's transformation: in Christ, the need for all these institutions is fulfilled and transcended. There is no temple because God and the Lamb are its temple (Revelation 21:22); no king but the King of kings; no sacrifice because the once-for-all offering has been made (Hebrews 9:26-28); no ephod because the Spirit gives direct access to divine guidance. The suspension of the old institutions prepares for the installation of the new -- Christ as the fulfillment of each.
5: The promise that Israel will 'seek the LORD their God and David their king' in the latter days identifies the messianic king as the one who reunites the repentant people. The NT identifies Jesus as this Davidic king: 'He will be great and will be called the Son of the Most High. And the Lord God will give to him the throne of his father David' (Luke 1:32); 'Is not this the Christ, the descendant of David, who comes from Bethlehem?' (John 7:42). The trembling approach to YHWH and his goodness in the latter days is fulfilled for all who come to Christ in repentance: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matthew 11:28). Christ is the David their king for whom Hosea's Israel waits.
